text: The surgeon places laparoscopic ports in the right hemiabdomen, standing on the patient's right side and facing left. Upon entry into the abdomen, there is visible Kyle pooling within the abdominal cavity and between the mesenteric leaflets.
The surgeon identifies the terminal ileum and the leaf of Treves, using a retrograde approach along the common channel to reduce the small intestine and locate the internal hernia.

The surgeon reduces the small intestine by walking up the common channel, revealing lymph trapped within the mesenteric leaflets and visible venous congestion. The reduction continues until reaching the roux limb, which is then followed to the gastrojejunostomy site.
The surgeon systematically examines the jejunojejunostomy area and checks between the mesenteric leaflets for internal hernias, noting that the hernia in this location has been sutured closed.

The surgeon flips the Roux limb to the left, revealing the space between the Roux mesentery and the transverse colon. A large mesenteric defect, identified as Peterson's hernia, is visible and is sutured closed using a non-absorbable suture.
The surgeon completes the closure of the Peterson's defect using sutures. The bowel is visually inspected by running it to confirm the absence of additional abnormalities.
